title: Emergency Food Sources
summary: Finding food in emergency situations when supplies run out
body:
## The Survival Priority Order

Before expending energy on food procurement, remember the **Rule of Threes**:

- 3 minutes without air
- 3 hours without shelter (in harsh conditions)
- 3 days without water
- **3 weeks without food**

Food is the **lowest survival priority**. Address shelter and water first. Searching for food burns calories you may not be able to replace, so be strategic rather than desperate.

## Universal Edibility Test

When you encounter an unknown plant and have no identification resources, this systematic test can help determine if it is safe to eat. **Each step must be completed fully before proceeding.**

1. **Separate the plant** into parts: leaves, stems, roots, flowers, and fruit. Test only one part at a time.
2. **Smell test** - Crush the plant part and smell it. Discard anything that smells strongly of almonds (possible cyanide) or has a very bitter, soapy odor.
3. **Skin contact test** - Rub the crushed plant on the inside of your wrist or elbow. Wait **8 hours**. If there is no rash, itching, burning, or swelling, proceed.
4. **Lip test** - Touch the plant part to the corner of your lip. Wait **15 minutes**. If there is no reaction, proceed.
5. **Tongue test** - Place the plant part on your tongue without chewing. Wait **15 minutes**. If there is no numbness, burning, or unpleasant reaction, proceed.
6. **Chew test** - Chew a small amount and hold it in your mouth for **15 minutes** without swallowing. Spit it out if any burning or unpleasant reaction occurs.
7. **Swallow test** - Swallow a very small portion. Wait **8 hours** without eating anything else. If there is no nausea, cramps, or diarrhea, the plant part is likely safe in small quantities.

**WARNING: This test requires nearly 24 hours per plant part. It is a last resort, not a shortcut.**

## Safe Insect Food

Insects are the most accessible and calorie-dense wild food source available almost everywhere.

- **Grasshoppers and crickets** - Remove legs (barbed) and wings before eating. Cook if possible (skewer and roast over coals) to kill parasites. High in protein.
- **Ants** - Edible raw or cooked. Drop into water to collect easily. Avoid fire ants.
- **Grubs and larvae** - Found under bark and in rotting wood. High in fat and calories. Cook to improve taste and safety.
- **Earthworms** - Purge by squeezing out gut contents or placing in clean water for several hours. Cook before eating.

**AVOID**: Brightly colored insects (warning coloration), hairy caterpillars, insects that smell bad when crushed, ticks, flies, and mosquitoes. Bright colors in nature often signal toxins.

## Reliable Wild Plants

These plants are widespread and commonly identifiable:

- **Dandelion** - Entire plant is edible: leaves (salad green), roots (roast as coffee substitute), flowers (eat raw). Found on every continent except Antarctica.
- **Cattail** - Often called the "supermarket of the swamp." Young shoots eaten like celery, starchy roots can be processed into flour, flower heads roasted and eaten like corn. Found near any freshwater marsh.
- **Inner bark (cambium)** - The soft, moist layer between the bark and wood of pine, birch, willow, and poplar trees is edible. Scrape it off in strips with a knife. Can be eaten raw, boiled, or dried and ground into flour. High in calories and available year-round.

## Simple Snare Principles

Trapping is far more energy-efficient than active hunting:

- Place snares on **game trails** (narrow paths with tracks and droppings)
- Use a **simple loop snare**: Form a noose from cordage or wire, sized to the target animal's head. Hang it at head height across a trail.
- Set **multiple snares** - Success rate per snare is low, so set as many as possible and check them every 12-24 hours
- Funnel animals toward snares using **guide sticks** angled to narrow the path
- Small game (rabbits, squirrels) provides the best calorie return for effort in most environments

## Critical Warnings

- **NEVER eat wild mushrooms** without expert identification. Many deadly species closely resemble edible ones, and some can cause fatal liver failure days after ingestion with no initial symptoms.
- **Never eat plants with milky sap** (except dandelion) unless positively identified as safe.
- **Avoid plants with umbrella-shaped flower clusters** (could be poison hemlock or water hemlock, among the most toxic plants in North America).
- **Cook all animal food** when possible to kill parasites and bacteria.
- **Conserve energy** - If foraging efforts burn more calories than you gain, stop and focus on signaling for rescue.
